one_line_takeaway: UNCLEAN shows that spreading an imperceptible, frequency-domain backdoor trigger across the target class AND carefully selected dissimilar forget-set samples lets a fully clean (unpoisoned) unlearning request itself activate and amplify the backdoor, defeating forget-set filtering defenses and beating a prior clean-unlearning attack's post-unlearning ASR by roughly 32 percentage points on CIFAR-10.
key_finding: Across all four datasets and five unlearning strategies, ASR increases substantially after unlearning relative to before -- e.g., on CIFAR-10 with Fisher Forgetting, ASR rises from 39.1% before unlearning to 92.7% after (Table I row combining pre/post baseline and Table V 'Unclean' row); on MNIST, ASR rises from 27.7% to 88.2%, and on FashionMNIST from 26.5% to 93.4% (Table V, Unclean scenario). Fisher Forgetting and Boundary Unlearning show the highest post-unlearning vulnerability (e.g., Fisher Forgetting reaches 88.2% ASR on MNIST, 93.4% on FashionMNIST, 92.7% on CIFAR-10, 91.7% on STL-10 in Table I). The attack preserves Acc Retain and drives Acc Forget toward 0% comparably to clean (unattacked) unlearning (Table II), showing it does not disrupt normal unlearning utility. Against defenses (Table III, using Fisher Forgetting), the attack remains largely resilient to Neural Cleanse and Cognitive Distillation (ASR often improves, e.g., FashionMNIST reaches 99.8% ASR under Neural Cleanse), with Neural Cleanse failing to identify the true backdoored class in all runs; only I-BAU meaningfully reduces (but does not eliminate) ASR, e.g., dropping FashionMNIST ASR to 58.1%. Cross-architecture transfer (Figure 6) shows the attack generalizes when the trigger is generated on ResNet-18 but tested on VGG11, AllCNN, ViT, and ResNet-50, with ViT showing the highest ASR (up to 99.9% on MNIST). Compared directly against the prior clean-unlearning attack of Liu et al. [6] under matched settings (CIFAR-10, 5% poisoning, ResNet and VGG), UNCLEAN improves post-unlearning ASR from 45.0% to 83.3% (ResNet) and from 64.8% to 91.4% (VGG) -- the paper's headline '32% improvement' claim (Table IV). The ablation study (Table V) shows that removing the distant-sample selection strategy (Random Selection scenarios) has the largest negative impact, collapsing the pre/post-unlearning ASR gap (e.g., MNIST RS: 100% before, 100% after -- no unlearning-triggered jump), confirming that dissimilarity-based forget-set selection, not just the frequency trigger, is essential to the attack's core 'clean unlearning activates dormant backdoor' effect.
core_contribution: The paper introduces UNCLEAN (UNlearning-activated CLEAN backdoor attack), a two-phase backdoor attack against machine unlearning systems. Unlike prior work (Liu et al. [6]), which injects a weak, targeted backdoor trigger only into the target class during training and amplifies it via clean unlearning of the same class, UNCLEAN (1) generates a stealthy trigger in the frequency domain via a learnable, sigmoid-bounded, DCT-based perturbation confined to a mid-frequency band, using a shadow model and a cross-entropy loss to optimize the trigger against a target label; (2) embeds this weak trigger with clean labels into BOTH the target class and a deliberately selected, dissimilar subset of the forget-set (chosen by cosine similarity of shadow-model embeddings to the target-class average embedding, picking the least-similar/most-distant samples), spreading a non-targeted malicious signal across multiple classes rather than a single target class; and (3) exploits a completely clean unlearning phase (the forget-set submitted for unlearning contains only unmodified, non-triggered samples) so that when the model is asked to forget these samples, gradient realignment during unlearning removes the trigger's association with the forget-set's clean labels while preserving and strengthening the trigger-to-target-label association, causing the attack success rate (ASR) to sharply increase only after unlearning, not before.
threat_model: The attacker controls the training data source and has access to the clean training dataset used to train the target model, but only to partial data from the target class and a subset of data from the other classes (from which the forget-set is drawn) -- a partial/realistic knowledge setting rather than full white-box access to the deployed model. The attacker constructs a poisoned dataset by adding an imperceptible frequency-domain trigger to some images in the target class and a subset of the forget-set while keeping their original (clean) labels, then later submits a forget-set of entirely unaltered, clean data for unlearning. No poisoning of the unlearning/forget request itself occurs, distinguishing this from 'poisoned unlearning' attacks (Zhang et al. [7], Huang et al.'s UBA-Inf [8]) and making the attack undetectable by defenses that inspect or filter forget-set requests for anomalies.
limitations: All evaluation is on image classification benchmarks (MNIST, FashionMNIST, CIFAR-10, STL-10) with CNN/ViT architectures; no evaluation on other modalities (text, tabular, LLMs) or on larger-scale/real-world unlearning deployments. The attacker's access assumption (control over the clean training data source with only partial access to the target class and forget-set candidates) is stated as 'more realistic' than full poisoning access but is still a data-contributor threat model, not evaluated against production ML pipelines with data provenance controls. Comparison to Liu et al. [6] relies on the paper's own reimplementation since '[6]... does not provide an official code,' introducing potential reproduction fidelity uncertainty acknowledged implicitly by the authors. Only three defenses are tested (Neural Cleanse, Cognitive Distillation, I-BAU), all training-phase backdoor defenses; unlearning-specific or forget-request-auditing defenses are not evaluated (deliberately, since the whole premise is that the forget-set is clean and thus undetectable by such defenses, but this also means the paper does not explore whether other classes of defense, e.g., differential privacy in unlearning or provenance tracking, would mitigate the attack). The paper states it 'plan[s] to test empirically' broader universal-trigger transferability claims, indicating some generalization claims are partly speculative/future work rather than fully demonstrated.

quantitative_results: CIFAR-10, Fisher Forgetting: ASR before unlearning 39.1% -> after unlearning 92.7% (Table V, Unclean row)
MNIST, Fisher Forgetting: ASR before 27.7% -> after 88.2% (Table V / Table I)
FashionMNIST, Fisher Forgetting: ASR before 26.5% -> after 93.4%
STL-10, Fisher Forgetting: ASR before 27.4% -> after 91.7%
Comparison with Liu et al. [6] on CIFAR-10, 5% poisoning: ResNet ASR after unlearning 45.0% (Liu) vs 83.3% (UNCLEAN); VGG ASR after unlearning 64.8% (Liu) vs 91.4% (UNCLEAN) (Table IV)

Defense evaluation (Fisher Forgetting, Table III): Neural Cleanse leaves ASR at 68.1% (MNIST) to 99.8% (FashionMNIST) to 87.7% (CIFAR-10) to 98.5% (STL-10); Cognitive Distillation leaves ASR 89.1%/85.6%/88.6%/83.3% across the same datasets; I-BAU reduces ASR most, to 71.0%/58.1%/59.4%/56.9%

Cross-architecture transfer (Figure 6): ASR ranges roughly 73.4%-99.9% across VGG11, AllCNN, ViT, ResNet50 when trigger generated on ResNet18, with ViT reaching up to 99.9% (MNIST) and 99.5%/99.8% (STL10/FashionMNIST)

Ablation (Table V): full UNCLEAN attack yields the largest before/after ASR gap (e.g., MNIST 27.7% -> 88.2%), whereas Random Selection scenario collapses the gap (e.g., MNIST 100% -> 100%), showing data-selection strategy is the dominant contributor
Overall headline claim: 'improvement in the attack success rate of more than 32% compared to the previous work by [6]'
baselines_compared: Liu et al. 2024 (AAAI) 'Backdoor attacks via machine unlearning' -- clean unlearning backdoor attack targeting a single class, direct head-to-head comparison in Table IV
Zhang et al. 2023 'Backdoor attack through machine unlearning' -- poisoned-unlearning attack, discussed as related but not directly benchmarked
Huang et al. 2024 (USENIX Security) UBA-Inf -- influence-driven camouflage poisoned-unlearning attack, discussed as related but not directly benchmarked

Five unlearning strategies used as the underlying mechanism being attacked (not baselines to UNCLEAN itself): Bad Teacher, Fisher Forgetting, Boundary Unlearning, Gradient Ascent, Random Label unlearning
Three backdoor defenses tested for robustness: Neural Cleanse, Cognitive Distillation, I-BAU (Implicit Backdoor Adversarial Unlearning)
